findings.title: MarkItDown rapid GitHub adoption (87K stars)
findings.summary: MarkItDown reached 87,000 GitHub stars by April 2026, gaining 25,000 stars within two weeks of December 2024 launch. Growth is attributed to Microsoft brand recognition rather than sustained technical community recommendation.
findings.invalidation_condition: No invalidation expected; adoption metrics are historical fact.
findings.decay_rationale: Historical adoption metrics do not age; star count is permanent record.

findings.title: 29+ format support with uneven quality
findings.summary: MarkItDown supports 29+ file formats (PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, HTML, images, audio, ZIP, EPUB, RSS, YouTube). Support quality varies: Office documents 65–85% success; mixed-format 47.3%; PDFs 30–40%. Marketing emphasizes breadth; reality shows concentrated strength in Office documents only.
findings.invalidation_condition: Major MarkItDown release improving PDF handling to 0.7+ heading/table scores would change quality distribution.
findings.decay_rationale: Success rates depend on MarkItDown version (0.1.5 had breaking changes). Re-test quarterly on latest version.

findings.title: PDF handling failure: 0.589 overall, 0.000 heading hierarchy, 0.273 tables
findings.summary: OpenDataLoader benchmark (12 engines, standardized dataset): MarkItDown 0.589/1.0 overall. Heading hierarchy 0.000 (all text flattened, no structure). Table fidelity 0.273 (cells extracted as sequential text, relationships destroyed). Speed 0.114 sec/page is irrelevant given output quality.
findings.invalidation_condition: New OpenDataLoader benchmark showing MarkItDown heading score >0.7 would invalidate this finding.
findings.decay_rationale: PDF conversion algorithms evolve with releases. Re-benchmark with each v0.x release.

findings.title: Wrapper architecture on unmaintained libraries
findings.summary: MarkItDown is unified API to existing libraries: mammoth (Word, unmaintained since 2018), pandas (Excel), python-pptx (PowerPoint), pdfminer.six (PDF), BeautifulSoup (HTML). Mammoth maintenance risk is single point of failure for DOCX support.
findings.invalidation_condition: Mammoth active maintenance resumed, or MarkItDown replaces with maintained library, would change risk profile.
findings.decay_rationale: Maintenance status of upstream dependencies (mammoth) can change without warning. Re-check mammoth GitHub monthly.

findings.title: Encoding crashes on real-world documents
findings.summary: GitHub issues #291, #1290, #138 document UnicodeEncodeError crashes on production files. Issue #291: 'Crashes on every file i tested (more than 100).' Windows systems particularly vulnerable. Recent versions (post-0.1.5) still exhibit bugs.
findings.invalidation_condition: Production deployment reports show <1% encoding crash rate on real-world documents would suggest GitHub issues are edge cases.
findings.decay_rationale: Encoding bugs are version-specific. Re-test with each release on Windows production-like datasets.

findings.title: Docling 0.882 score with 97.9% table accuracy
findings.summary: Docling (IBM, MIT) achieves 0.882/1.0 OpenDataLoader with 0.824 heading, 0.887 tables. Real-world test: 100% text accuracy, 97.9% cell-level accuracy on complex hierarchical tables (5 reports, 3–32 tables). Trade-off: 1GB+ models, slower (0.45–6.28s CPU).
findings.invalidation_condition: Docling regression or new competitor with same accuracy at MarkItDown speed would reduce Docling's relative appeal.
findings.decay_rationale: Docling released July 2024 with active development. Benchmark scores may improve/regress with releases. Re-test quarterly.

findings.title: 47.3% success rate on mixed-format real-world documents
findings.summary: DEV.to benchmark on 120 real-world files (DOCX, PPTX, HTML, CSV): 47.3% first-pass success. Means ~52.7% require fallback handling or error recovery. Office-only tests higher (65–85%); PDF-heavy tests lower (30–40%).
sources: DEV.to: I benchmarked 4 Python text extraction libraries (2025)
findings.invalidation_condition: Independent benchmark on larger/different document set showing >70% success would suggest test bias.
findings.decay_rationale: Success rate depends on document source, MarkItDown version, and system configuration. Requires periodic real-world testing.

findings.title: Office documents (DOCX, PPTX, XLSX) are MarkItDown's actual strength
findings.summary: Real-world breakdown: Office 65–85%, mixed-format 47.3%, PDFs 30–40%. Yage.ai: 'If mainly dealing with Office documents, reasonable choice; if PDFs, use something else.' Marketing narrative 'universal converter' masks reality: 'Office specialist.'
findings.invalidation_condition: Major improvements to PDF handling or deprecation of Office format support would change this finding.
findings.decay_rationale: Office format support is mature; unlikely to change dramatically. Verify annually for major architectural shifts.

findings.title: Fallback pipeline required for production LLM ingestion
findings.summary: 47.3% success rate on mixed documents means ~50% require alternative handling. Production RAG requires hybrid: MarkItDown (Office) + fallback tool (Docling, Marker) for PDFs. Engineering overhead and latency costs not fully quantified.
findings.invalidation_condition: MarkItDown success rate improvement to >80% on mixed formats would reduce fallback necessity.
findings.decay_rationale: Fallback strategy depends on MarkItDown version and fallback tool selection. Architecture review quarterly.

findings.title: Vision-language models emerging as alternative to OCR
findings.summary: GPT-4V, Claude 3, Gemini, Qwen 2.5-VL achieve competitive/superior accuracy to OCR. Gemini Flash 2.0: 6000 pages for $1 (8x cheaper than traditional OCR). VLMs shift document handling from file-to-text to end-to-end visual understanding.
findings.invalidation_condition: VLM hallucination on structured documents (tables, forms) increases, or API costs rise substantially, would reduce appeal.
findings.decay_rationale: VLM technology, pricing, and performance are rapidly evolving. Re-evaluate quarterly on new model releases.

findings.title: Installation footprint trade-off: MarkItDown 71MB vs. Docling 1GB+
findings.summary: MarkItDown ~71MB with optional dependencies. Docling 1GB+ (models downloaded on first use from Hugging Face). Lightweight converters suit APIs; heavy models suit offline batch. No universal solution.
findings.invalidation_condition: Package optimization (Docling model compression, MarkItDown optional dependency growth) would change footprint trade-off.
findings.decay_rationale: Package sizes change with releases. Re-verify actual `pip install` footprint with each major version.

findings.title: Plugin architecture (v0.1.0+) enables third-party extensions
findings.summary: v0.1.0+ uses Python entry points for custom DocumentConverter plugins (e.g., markitdown-ocr for OCR). Extensibility valuable for specialized cases, but plugin ecosystem maturity unknown; no comprehensive directory exists.
findings.invalidation_condition: Mature plugin ecosystem emerges with vetted, well-maintained third-party plugins.
findings.decay_rationale: Plugin ecosystem is nascent (v0.1.0 ~6 months old). Requires periodic ecosystem survey.

must_carry_caveats.text: MarkItDown unsuitable for PDF-centric LLM pipelines without fallback conversion tool (Docling, Marker).
must_carry_caveats.reason: PDF handling score 0.589/1.0 with 0.000 heading hierarchy means no document structure preservation—critical for RAG retrieval quality and LLM training data preparation.

must_carry_caveats.text: 47.3% success rate on mixed-format real-world documents; ~50% require fallback handling or error recovery.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Production pipelines relying solely on MarkItDown will encounter failures on half of typical document loads. Fallback infrastructure is architectural requirement, not optional.

must_carry_caveats.text: Heading hierarchy 0.000 means all text flattened to single level; table extraction 0.273/1.0 functionally broken.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Structure loss is deal-breaker for LLM applications. Document meaning depends on hierarchical relationships, not just text. MarkItDown output is unsuitable for fine-tuning or RAG without post-processing.

must_carry_caveats.text: Encoding crashes reproducible on Windows (GitHub #291: '100+ files,' issue #1290 recent). Production deployment requires error handling and fallback.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Unicode/codepage issues on Windows represent operational risk. Systems without try/catch and retry logic will experience downtime on real-world document sets.

must_carry_caveats.text: Non-English document performance unverified; independent testing required before deployment in multilingual environments.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Encoding issues suggest potential problems with CJK, Arabic, RTL text. Benchmarks are English-only. Tools like MinerU noted as superior for CJK. Cannot assume parity.

must_carry_caveats.text: Fallback tool (Docling, Marker) required for production mixed-format pipelines; cost and latency trade-offs depend on document volume and infrastructure constraints.
must_carry_caveats.reason: Total cost of ownership calculation requires modeling dual-tool setup engineering, infrastructure, and operational overhead. No generic formula; requires custom analysis per organization.

staleness_summary.decay_rationale: Vision-language models evolve monthly with new releases, API cost changes, and performance benchmarks. Recommendation for VLM alternative to MarkItDown/OCR requires quarterly review.

contradiction_1.resolution: Both correct; different scopes. Format support ≠ quality. Addressed in F2 with success rate breakdown.

contradiction_2.resolution: Use-case dependent. For LLM quality, accuracy dominates. Addressed in F3 with benchmark context.

contradiction_3.resolution: Both correct. Capability exists; reliability (47.3% success) insufficient alone. Addressed in F9.

source_bias_flags: Microsoft marketing halo effect on GitHub stars (brand recognition, not technical merit)
Benchmark presentation bias: MarkItDown 0.589 presented as 'reasonable' without emphasizing heading 0.000 complete failure
Selection bias in use-case framing: Marketing focuses on Office documents (success cases); PDF limitations buried in GitHub issues
methodology_concerns: PDF benchmark scope: 12 engines on curated test set. Real-world PDFs (scanned, handwritten, domain-specific) may perform differently.
Single-source on success rates: DEV.to benchmark on 120 files. Needs replication on larger dataset.
Plugin ecosystem assessment: Nascent (v0.1.0 ~6 months). Cannot assess long-term maturity.
quality_notes.coverage_confidence: HIGH on product limitations (benchmarked, reproducible). MEDIUM on market positioning and adoption patterns (limited case study evidence).
